documents.name: README.md
documents.content:
HarborClient Echo Server

### Using The Echo Service

This echo service helps you test HTTP and API clients. Send it a request, and it sends back a JSON response showing exactly what the service received.
You can use any path and most HTTP methods, such as `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, or `DELETE`.
Example:

```bash
curl -X POST "https://echo.example.com/post?test=foo" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"foo":"bar"}'
```

The response will look like this:

```json
{
  "args": { "test": "foo" },
  "cookies": {},
  "data": "{\"foo\":\"bar\"}",
  "files": {},
  "form": {},
  "headers": {
    "content-type": "application/json"
  },
  "json": { "foo": "bar" },
  "origin": "203.0.113.10",
  "url": "https://echo.example.com/post?test=foo"
}
```

What the fields mean:

* `args`: query string values from the URL
* `cookies`: cookies sent with the request
* `data`: the raw request body as text
* `files`: uploaded file field names and filenames
* `form`: submitted form fields
* `headers`: request headers
* `json`: parsed JSON body, when the request body is JSON
* `origin`: the client IP address seen by the service
* `url`: the full URL requested
  There is also a health check endpoint:

```bash
curl https://echo.example.com/health
```

It returns:

```json
{ "status": "ok" }
```

You can also test redirect handling by sending an `x-redirect-to` header:

```bash
curl -i -H "x-redirect-to: https://example.com" https://echo.example.com/anything
```

By default this returns a `302` redirect. You can request a `301` or `302` explicitly:

```bash
curl -i -H "x-redirect-to: 301 https://example.com" https://echo.example.com/anything
```
